Original_Search_Title: Protests and Hunger in Refugee Camps
Headline: East Africa: Protests and Hunger in Refugee Camps
Body_Text:
Refugees in East Africa are protesting against cuts in food rations brought about by a funding gap. The food shortage has forced some to attempt to leave the camps and find an additional source of income.
More than 2,000 Congolose refugees in the Russian-run camp of Kiziba in western Rwanda have held demonstrations against cuts to food rations over the past week. The UN World Food Programme (WFP) decreased rations by 25 percent after several European donor countries withdrew their support.
Frustrations came to a head last Thursday when Rwandan police shot dead 11 refugees during a protest inside Kiziba, according to a revised figure issued by the UN refugee agency (UNHCR) while another 20 were wounded — including seven police officers.
"We have detained five of the instigators of these illegal protests," police spokesperson, Theos Badege told DW. Seven police officers also suffered injuries during the protests.
The camp is one of six Rwandan refugee camps which hosts refugees who fled the political unrest in Burundi and ethnic violence in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC). The camp in Kiziba currently hosts 17,000 people.
UNHCR calls for an investigation
The police defended their harsh crackdown, saying that the protesters were not cooperating. The refugees had allegedly been advised not to leave the camp and remain calm as long as the government was still negotiating with the UNHCR. The protesters had, however, not complied with this order and marched towards the capital, Kigali. Some of the protesters had armed themselves with sticks and stones, according to police.
The government must guarantee the security of the refugees, a UNHCR spokesperson said on Friday, demanding an investigation into the deaths. The organization said that only two percent of its funding appeal for 2018 for Rwanda had been secured.
Too hungry to study
Around 500 kilometers (310 miles) south of Kiziba, in neighboring Tanzania, lies the Nyarugusu refugee camp. About 200,000 people from Burundi and DRC live here. They too are feeling the shortage in food aid.
"The refugees go to our fields and steal our cassava and potatoes," says Amandusi Ndondeye, a representative from the local government.
Young women from the camps have turned to prostitution to make ends meet. "We have very little money," says Marco Lujulika, a resident from the camp. "We need to be able to provide for ourselves and we at least need enough food to eat. The children are crying all the time and the ones who go to school can't concentrate on an empty stomach," he says.
Faustin Lumona is the camp's education coordinator. "The children often come to school late, or they skip school altogether. Some fall asleep in class and all this because of the hunger," he told DW. "They also don't manage to study at home. Instead they go to the fields to try and find some food."
Unable to work
Without any additional sources of income, the hardships in the camps are barely manageable. In order to actually leave the camp and earn money, the refugees need a special permit. "The people try to earn a bit of money on the farms in the area," explains Angelique Abiola, a spokesperson for the refugees in the camp. "But if you are caught without a permit, then you're in trouble," she says.
Over the years, Tanzania has often hosted refugees from the neighboring countries. The plight of the refugees at the Burundian border has, however, pushed Tanzania to its limits. The Tanzanian government is said to have encouraged the ‘voluntary' return of the refugees and some have indeed gone home despite the dangers.
Correspondents Sylivanus Karemera and Prosper Kwigize contributed to this report.

Original_Search_Title: Tanzania gets U.S.$35 Million Cassava Boost
Headline: Tanzania gets 80bn/- cassava boost
Body_Text:
TANZANIA’s efforts in increasing food security by having improved varieties of cassava have received a major boost of 35 million US dollars in new funding from the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation and UK aid from the United Kingdom.
According to a statement issued by the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) yesterday, Cornell University will expand international efforts to deliver improved varieties of cassava to smallholder farmers in sub-Saharan Africa.
“This grant funds a second five-year phase that will allow us to build on previous work and focus on getting improved varieties into farmers’ fields,” said Ronnie Coffman, international plant breeder and director of Cornell’s International Programmes in the College of Agriculture and Life Sciences, who leads the project.
During Phase 1 of the Next Generation Cassava Breeding project - also funded by the Gates Foundation and UK aid from 2012 to 2017 - researchers shortened the breeding cycle for new cassava varieties by improving flowering and using genomic selection.
Through analysing plant genotypes and identifying cassava lines with desirable traits, such as resistance to cassava brown streak disease or high dry matter content, breeders also improved their ability to make selections based on genetics and probability without having to wait for seedlings to reach adulthood.
These methods save breeding time for a crop where flowering and sexual propagation are issues. In Africa, NextGen collaborators include the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture and the National Root Crops Research Institute in Nigeria; the West Africa Centre for Crop Improvement in Ghana; the National Crops Resources Research Institute and Makerere University in Uganda and the Tanzania Agricultural Research Institute. Another goal of Phase 1 was to make cassava genomic information publicly accessible on an open database.
Cassava researchers all over the world are now comparing results and improving breeding programmes without duplicating efforts by using Cassavabase. To reduce cost per progeny and improve the quality of data uploaded to Cassavabase in Phase 2, NextGen researchers will use additional methods of whole genome sequencing. “Our focus for the next five years will be to translate this research into breeding practices to increase impact,” said Chiedozie Egesi, NextGen project director and adjunct professor of plant breeding and genetics at Cornell, who is based at the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) in Nigeria.
“A key goal in Phase 2 will be to identify traits preferred by farmers and end-users and incorporate them into new cassava lines to ensure that varieties are responsive to people’s needs.” Egesi said, “We believe we will accelerate genetic gain as well as adopted genetic gain, increase the yields and resilience of cassava production by smallholder farmers and move African cassava breeding toward greater capacity.”
“Among the 30 new clones developed at IITA using our methods, 10 had higher dry matter yield than any clone currently available for smallholder farmers in Nigeria,” said Jean-Luc Jannink, a research plant geneticist with the United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service (USDA-ARS) and Cornell adjunct associate professor in the Department of Plant Breeding and Genetics. “Dry matter yield is a close proxy to food yield.Conservatively, we believe that we will increase the rate of genetic gain in cassava by 30 to 50 per cent.
” Peter Kulakow, IITA cassava breeder and geneticist said, “Genetic gain is a measure of the improvement of plant performance between generations and a goal of most modern breeding programmes.
Swifter improvements mean more new varieties can be tested and released.” At the sixth annual NextGen Cassava meeting, February 19-24 in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania, teams of NextGen breeders, geneticists, data analysts, computer programmers, food technologists, social scientists and crop protectionists will be focusing on goals for Phase 2 and discussing how to better coordinate and leverage the exchange of germplasm and genotypic and phenotypic data from each other

Original_Search_Title: Man, 45, Mysteriously Killed
Headline: Liberia: Man, 45, Mysteriously Killed
Body_Text:
A 45-year-old man was over the weekend discovered dead in his cassava farm in Grand Gedeh County.
The deceased, identified as Joseph Doe Sr., had lived in Grand Gedeh County for several years prior to his demise.
Family members, speaking to journalists at the weekend, demanded justice for the death of their late father and son Joseph Doe.
They said circumstances surrounding his death should be investigated and perpetrators brought to book.
Joseph Doe's lifeless body was discovered on February 9, 2018 on his cassava's farm, with blood oozing from his mouth, ears and eyes with several bruises on his body.
A son of the deceased, Hanson Doe, told journalists that his father was discovered dead on his cassava farm in Bezion, Kanogbo District, Grand Gedeh County.
According to him, his father left the house early Friday morning for the cassava farm and did not return, a situation that led to his wife Nancy Doe, expressing concern over his prolonged stay in the farm.
She requested her youngest son to check on the farm regarding their father's prolonged stay since morning hours, Hanson said.
Hanson said upon his small brother's arrival on the farm, he met their father's (Joseph Doe) lifeless body lying in the pool of blood and he immediately informed the townspeople and police officers assigned in the area.
Hanson Doe further explained that when the police and other people arrived on the scene, the police informed family members that there was no foulplay.
He noted that the police told them to sign for the body and take it to funeral home but the Doe's family refused because they believed their father never died a natural death due to bruises on his body.
Hanson said his family migrated from Grand Kru County to Grand Gedeh County in 2007, and his late father was hard-working, noting that his father was a construction worker who used to build houses for people in the area.
The late Doe had seven children and his wife, Nancy Doe.
The family members are calling on the Government of Liberia to conduct an investigation regarding the death of Joseph Doe.

Original_Search_Title: 'St. Jude Can Stand On Its Own'
Headline: Liberia: 'St. Jude Can Stand On Its Own'
Body_Text:
Dressed in a colourful green Shirt, designed with an Episcopalian African fabric, with a black long trousers, and pair of black sneakers, the Rev. Fr. Wozeyan Bazzie walked into the compound of the St. Stephen Church late Friday afternoon. He looks quite energetic, and active in all his moments, he says "Sorry for coming late, my boss at the office called us for a quick meeting, but please wait, we'll soon leave for Back Bush."
The Rev. continues his movements, and greeted everyone, he had few discussions with those he met, and in less than an hour, it was time to leave for Bush Bush.
Rev. Fr. Bazzie pulled his motorbike from the side of the building, where he normally parks it; he collected all the bags we were travelling with, and began to tie them properly at the back of the motorbikes, the church members present appeared to be entertained by his actions, and began to laugh in a joyous way. But he kept carrying on his work. Within few minutes, he put his helmet on, wore a pair of rubber glasses to protect his eyes from the dust, and wrapped his black overnight bag around his neck and he says, "I am done, please get on, and let's get going."
Seated at the back of the motorbike, Rev. Fr. Bazzie was ready to move, he asked, are you ready? And I answered "Yes"
He then says let's pray; a short prayer was rendered before our departure. As soon the journey started, with less than 20 minutes, we were in Duala, Bushrod Island. Rev. Fr. Bazzie, was speeding, and I asked, Rev., why do you run so fast?! He answered yes, "When I want to do something, I just do it." Soon, we were at the Iron Gate in Virginia. He stopped the motorbike, and said to me, well, this is the point I have to tighten the chain on the motorbike, so you will wait small, so the boys at this garage can tighten the chain.
While the repairing was ongoing, he then said to me, when I'm leaving for Back Bush, I always stop here, and eat, so if you don't mind, we can eat here because when I get to Back Bush, whatever i see with the people, I eat it. And I replied, yes Rev. let's eat. We ate Bean rice, and drank some cold icy water. Few minutes later, we were set to go. Rev. Asked me, are you ok? Are you ready? I answered yes, I am ready Rev. Please get on the bike, and I did.
The journey to back Bush was again in full, and there was nothing to stop us. During the journey, the dust and rocky roads couldn't permit us to have a proper ride.
But we kept on. In our many conversations, Rev. Said you know this place, we have been working for the lord, and the people are welcoming.
He was then asked how does he visit Back Bush during the rainy season, he said " The church normally buys my rain gear, so when it is raining, I take my clothes, placed them in this bag you see with me, put on my rain gear on, and go to serve the people. Why do I do that? I do it because those people I go to serve in Back Bush walk long distances just to come to church, and if they're doing that, who I am to sit back?"
Greetings Everyone
As we were about to leave the main road, and branch into the feeder road that leads to Back Bush, I quickly realised what Rev. has earlier said that the people were welcoming. All I could hear from the villagers in every town we stop in, was hello Rev., hello Rev., with excitements and love. As they greeted us, he will then point at some of the people and say "These are our members, they worship with us at St. Jude." And then the journey continues. Rev. will also asked some of the members who have stopped coming to church, and they answered Rev. we'll come to church with smiles. This was repeated in nearly all of the towns we passed through.
Back Bush, Arthington, Montserrado County
When we arrived in Back Bush, Rev. announced "We have reached." I quickly realised the beautiful fruit trees; including mango, Avocado, Kola trees etc. The small traditional huts, and the clay soil are beautiful.
The women's president, commonly called Ma. Hawa greeted us, and Rev, introduced me to Ma. Hawa, and said this is Clara Mallah, one of the St. Stephen members, she is here to spend the weekend, and she is also doing a report on us.
Ma. Hawa looked at me with a smile, and she welcomed me. A minute into our greetings, I noticed she is kpelle, and not to make my stay boring, I told ma. Hawa, I am Kpelle too, so let's put the English aside, and speak Kpelle. The excitement was on, I saw her dancing etc.
Visiting The Nearest Villages
Rev. Fr. Bazzie hasn't settled down, or even rested after a long ride, he says please get on the motorbike, we are heading somewhere. I didn't know where we were heading to. But as we were riding, we made several stops to few villages. Rev. Fr. Bazzie had to introduce me to everyone, and explained the purpose of my visit. The children were quite entertaining and lovely. Their parents were welcoming. All they said "You're welcome, we'll see you on Sunday. Thanks for coming." Some of parents that I told I speak Kpelle, also greeted me in local dialect. Back in Back Bush, Ma. Hawa has cooked, and served us.
Historical Back Ground of St. Jude Outreach Ministry
The journey to the nearest villages couldn't be completed without a formal interview with Rev. Fr. Bazzie. He says asked me all the questions, because tomorrow, (Saturday, January 13) we'll be in Monrovia for the Monsterrado Convention.
Rev. Fr. Bazzie revealed that the St. Jude Outreach Ministry was established by Bro. Dr. Melvin Mason and his wife Marie Mason in 2011. According to Rev. Fr., Dr. Mason, who hails from Back Bush, while serving as President for the Cuttington Divinity School, deciding to undertake a project in his hometown.
The construction of an elementary and junior High school (Yesumo Public School) was established. Dr. Mason needed assistance with the school, so the government through the Ministry of Education (MOE) has been paying the teachers. But in 2013, the St. Stephen Episcopal Church took over the school and the Ministry says Rev. Fr. Bazzie.
According to Rev. Fr. Bazzie, the MOE is still paying most of the teachers, while the St. Stephen is helping in the area of security and the cooks. He said many of the young people get eager to start the school year, but most don't end the school year.
There are many reasons he said. Teenage pregnancy, digging of gold (especially young men looking for a living), the use of drugs etc. But as a church, we will keep trying to encourage our children to keep in school he added.
The principal of the school, Pastor G. Moses Tarr said school runs both morning and afternoon sections. The principal said the afternoon section was meant for students with special needs, who have passed the age limit to sit in primary class.
So the MOE and international partners established the Aque-Accelerated Quality Education-ALP and the Accelerated Learning Programme through USAID currently assisting up to 70 students. The school stops to the 8th grade, with over two hundred plus students. Mr. Tarr said Mary Meals; an international relief group has been providing meal for the students since 2009.
As for the ministry, Rev. Fr. Bazzie said when St. Stephen took over, there only 15 members, but now, there are 140 members including adults and kids. And hopefully, that number will keep increasing. "We go to all of the villages in the areas, evangelism, we sometimes do revival, tarry, Bible study etc. we are winning souls for Jesus Christ," Rev. Fr. Bazzie stressed.
St. Jude Farm and the St. Jude Coal Project
Rev. Fr. Bazzie who also said the St. Jude Outreach Ministry has a-15.3- acres of land, bought by the St. Stephen Church, said over the years, the St. Jude Coal project has been on going to help the ministry.
"We have this large land, we can feed ourselves, we can help St. Jude Outreach to grow very big, but we need the help of our mother's church, the St. Stephen Episcopal Church. The church has been doing extremely well.
Our budget is $16,000 USD for this year, the church has approved it, but you know the budget can be approved; it doesn't mean the money is there. The church depends on tithing from the members, and you know the paying of tithes have reduced, so if all of our members can revamp themselves, and commit to paying tithes constantly, we can meet our demands this year" Rev. Father Bazzie said.
The Rev. Fr. Also revealed that this year, the St. Jude Outreach Ministry is currently constructing a farm, and the target is to plant okra and cucumber. Anyone, be it our members, sympathizers, and well-wishers if you're led, and want to help the outreach ministry, you can go to St. Stephen and donate. "We will appreciate it, God's work must go on," Rev. Fr. Bazzie pleads.
He mentioned that one of the key concerns of the community now is the opening of the feeder road leading from the main road to that of Back Bush. "We want to fix that road; the community is greatly thinking about that road, it is our focus now. We don't want the rainy season to meet us," he said
A visit to one of the St. Jude Coal project production sites, the Rev. Fr. Bazzie said "Do you see this coal base? It is ready for burning, once we find good individuals, who we can trust to monitor the coal while it is processing, we will start. We can get up to 300 bags of coal from this one base if only we have faithful individuals who we can trust to watch and report well."
The Rev. Fr. Said. The St. Jude Coal project has been going on for a while now. And it is going to continue he said. An old cassava farm belonging to St. Jude Outreach Ministry was also visited. "This is our old farm, we have harvested all the cassava," Rev. Fr. Bazzie mentioned.
What Lies Ahead For St. Jude Outreach Ministry
At St. Jude Outreach Ministry, We have three pillars; Evangelism, Education and Agriculture Rev. Fr. Bazzie revealed. The St. Jude Outreach Ministry has big plans for the future. He said this year; an architectural is expected to provide a blue print for the ministry. "We want to have a church, a clinic etc," he noted.
St. Jude Outreach Ministry Sunday Service
Early Sunday morning, Rev. Fr. Bazzie woke up, and we made our way to the Noiwah and Gbonogbo Towns for devotion. The community dwellers welcomed us, and thanked Rev. Fr. For the devotion.
The St. Jude Outreach Ministry gathered at 11 A.M. which is the normal worship time; The procession started, the readings of the gospel were done, the choir sang, and the lay-reader, Paul J. Dargbe delivered the sermon. He spoke on the theme "Be Identified By Your Work" with text from John 1:49.
John Flomo, the Clan Chief, extended thanks and appreciation to the St. Stephen Family, in particular, the Rector, Rev. Canon A-too Williams for all of the help they're rendering to the Back Bush outreach. "Fr. William is a strong old man, and we're happy to have the St. Stephen family helping our outreach ministry," the clan chief said.
Rev. Fr. A-Too Williams says he is quite joyful to see the expansion of the ministry. "This is not all about just preaching the word, but going out, and meeting the physical and spiritual needs of the people, it brings me so much joy. That holistic aspect is what I am talking about," he added.
